title: Stony Brook University — Wikipedia Overview
clean_text: Stony Brook University (SBU), officially the State University of New York at Stony Brook, is a public research university in Stony Brook, New York. Founded in 1957, it is one of four university centers of the State University of New York (SUNY) system. The university has a 1,454-acre main campus with additional facilities including Stony Brook Medicine (a major academic medical center), the Southampton campus, and Manhattan campuses. As of 2024, SBU enrolls approximately 27,252 students. The university's endowment was $724.4 million as of 2025. SBU is classified as an R1 doctoral university (very high research activity) by the Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education and is a member of the Association of American Universities (AAU). Current president is Andrea Goldsmith, who took office on August 1, 2025. Previous president was Maurie McInnis (2020–2025).

title: Stony Brook University — History
clean_text: Stony Brook University was established in 1957 as State University College on Long Island, with an initial focus on training mathematics and science teachers. It was originally located in Oyster Bay, New York. In 1962, the institution relocated to Stony Brook. The university was renamed State University of New York at Stony Brook in 1962. Under the leadership of John S. Toll (president 1965–1978), SBU transformed into a major research institution. In 2023, the Simons Foundation donated $500 million to SBU — the largest gift ever received by a public university in the United States — to create the Simons Institute for Science and Innovation. The university co-manages Brookhaven National Laboratory with Battelle Memorial Institute under contract with the U.S. Department of Energy.

title: Stony Brook University — Rankings and Reputation
clean_text: Stony Brook University is consistently ranked among the top public universities in the United States. In U.S. News & World Report's 2024–2025 rankings, SBU is ranked 58th among national universities and 26th among public universities. The university ranks in the top 100 globally according to QS World University Rankings, Times Higher Education World University Rankings, and the Academic Ranking of World Universities (Shanghai Ranking). SBU is a member of the Association of American Universities (AAU), an elite consortium of 71 leading research universities in North America. The university is recognized for excellence in STEM fields, medicine, and interdisciplinary research.

title: Stony Brook University — Academic Programs and Structure
clean_text: Stony Brook University is organized into colleges and schools: College of Arts and Sciences, College of Engineering and Applied Sciences (CEAS), College of Business, School of Medicine, School of Dental Medicine, School of Nursing, School of Health Technology and Management, School of Social Welfare, School of Marine and Atmospheric Sciences (SoMAS), and the Graduate School. SBU offers more than 200 undergraduate majors and minors, over 100 master's programs, and more than 50 doctoral programs. The Honors College provides enriched programs for academically exceptional undergraduates. SBU's academic calendar follows a semester system with fall (late August–December) and spring (January–May) semesters.

title: Stony Brook University — Research and Innovation
clean_text: Stony Brook University is a leading research institution with hundreds of millions in annual research expenditures. SBU co-manages Brookhaven National Laboratory, a U.S. Department of Energy facility on Long Island that has contributed to seven Nobel Prizes. Key SBU research contributions include MRI technology developed by Nobel laureate Paul Lauterbur. In 2023, the Simons Foundation pledged $500 million, the largest donation to a public university in U.S. history, to establish the Simons Institute for Science and Innovation at SBU. Research strengths include physics, chemistry, biology, medicine, neuroscience, artificial intelligence, climate science, and marine science. The Institute for AI-Driven Discovery and Innovation focuses on cutting-edge AI research and its interdisciplinary applications.

title: Stony Brook University — Nobel Laureates and Awards
clean_text: Stony Brook University has faculty and alumni who have won Nobel Prizes and other prestigious scientific awards. C.N. Yang (Chen-Ning Yang), Nobel Prize in Physics (1957), was a professor at Stony Brook. Paul Lauterbur, who developed MRI technology, won the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (2003) and was a faculty member at SBU. The Simons Center for Geometry and Physics at SBU has attracted numerous Fields Medalists and Nobel laureates. James Simons, the mathematics department founder and hedge fund billionaire, has provided major philanthropic support to the university through the Simons Foundation. Brookhaven National Laboratory, co-managed by SBU, produced research leading to Nobel Prizes in Physics for Samuel Ting (1976) and others.

title: Stony Brook University — Athletics
clean_text: Stony Brook University's athletic teams are known as the Seawolves. The university competes in NCAA Division I athletics. As of 2023, SBU is a member of the Coastal Athletic Association (CAA) for football and the America East Conference for most other sports. The university fields 19 varsity sports. The mascot is Wolfie the Seawolf. Official school colors are red, seawolf grey, and white. SBU's football team has been competitive in the Football Championship Subdivision (FCS) and has made NCAA FCS playoff appearances. The LaValle Stadium is the home of Seawolves football. Men's lacrosse, soccer, and track and field have also been strong programs historically.

title: Stony Brook University — Campus and Location
clean_text: Stony Brook University's main campus spans 1,454 acres in Stony Brook, New York, on Long Island's North Shore, approximately 60 miles east of Midtown Manhattan. The campus is accessible via the Long Island Rail Road (LIRR) Port Jefferson Branch — Stony Brook station is directly on campus. Key campus landmarks include the Stony Brook Union, the Frank Melville Jr. Memorial Library (main library), the Charles B. Wang Center (Asian-themed cultural center), the Simons Center for Geometry and Physics, the Institute for Advanced Computational Science (IACS), and Stony Brook Medicine hospital. The university operates a free campus loop bus system (Wolfie Bus). There is a Southampton campus focusing on environmental sciences. West Campus is the main academic area; Health Sciences campus houses the medical school and hospital.

title: Stony Brook University — Student Life
clean_text: Stony Brook University has a diverse student body representing all 50 U.S. states and over 100 countries, with approximately 27,252 students enrolled as of 2024. The university hosts more than 300 student clubs and organizations covering academic, cultural, athletic, professional, and social interests. Key student traditions include the Roth Pond Regatta (cardboard boat race), the Strawberry Festival, and annual cultural celebrations. The Student Activities Center (SAC) is the hub of campus life. Campus dining includes multiple residential dining halls and retail food options. On-campus housing accommodates thousands of students in various residence halls and apartment-style complexes. SBU's campus also includes a recreation center, natatorium, and outdoor athletic facilities.

title: Stony Brook Medicine — University Hospital
clean_text: Stony Brook Medicine is the integrated health system of Stony Brook University. It includes Stony Brook University Hospital, a 624-bed teaching hospital that is the only tertiary care center in Suffolk County, New York. Stony Brook Medicine also encompasses the Renaissance School of Medicine, School of Dental Medicine, School of Nursing, and various specialized care centers. The hospital serves as a regional trauma center (Level I Trauma Center designation). Stony Brook Medicine employs over 1,400 physicians and 7,000 healthcare professionals. It is consistently ranked among the best hospitals in New York State by U.S. News & World Report.

title: Stony Brook University — Presidents and Leadership
clean_text: Stony Brook University's presidents: John F. Brown (1960–1963), John S. Toll (1965–1978), John H. Marburger III (1980–1994), Shirley Strum Kenny (1994–2009), Samuel L. Stanley Jr. (2009–2020), Maurie McInnis (2020–2025), Andrea Goldsmith (2025–present). Andrea Goldsmith became the sixth president of Stony Brook University on August 1, 2025, making her the first woman to serve as president of SBU. She is an electrical engineering professor and a member of the National Academy of Engineering. The Provost serves as the chief academic officer. For current leadership, visit stonybrook.edu/president.

title: Notable Stony Brook University Alumni
clean_text: Notable Stony Brook University alumni include: Dario Amodei (CEO of Anthropic, AI safety researcher), John L. Hennessy (Stanford president, Turing Award winner, Google chair), Jef Raskin (creator of the Apple Macintosh interface), Joy Behar (comedian and co-host of The View), Sergey Brin's scientific advisor colleagues, Siobhan Magnus (American Idol contestant), Craig Newmark (founder of Craigslist — attended nearby), multiple U.S. senators and representatives, major medical researchers, and various technology industry executives. The Simons Center attracts internationally recognized mathematicians and physicists. SBU alumni work across medicine, technology, finance, public service, arts, and academia worldwide.

title: Notable Stony Brook University Faculty
clean_text: Notable Stony Brook University faculty include: C.N. Yang (Nobel Prize in Physics, 1957), Paul Lauterbur (Nobel Prize in Medicine, 2003, MRI inventor), James Simons (mathematician and founder of Renaissance Technologies, Simons Foundation), Peter Lax (Abel Prize 2005), Simon Donaldson (Fields Medal 1986), William Thurston (Fields Medal 1982), Cumrun Vafa (string theorist), Martin Liebeck, Olga Troyanskaya (computational biology), Michael Bender (algorithms), Klaus Mueller (visualization), Leman Akoglu (data mining), Steven Skiena (author of Algorithm Design Manual), Samir Das (networking), R. Sekar (cybersecurity). The CS faculty at SBU are internationally recognized in algorithms, AI, systems, security, HCI, and data science.

title: Stony Brook University and Brookhaven National Laboratory
clean_text: Stony Brook University co-manages Brookhaven National Laboratory (BNL) with Battelle Memorial Institute under a contract with the U.S. Department of Energy. BNL is located in Upton, New York, approximately 20 miles from SBU's main campus. Brookhaven is a multi-program national laboratory that has been the site of seven Nobel Prize-winning discoveries. Current major facilities at BNL include the Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider (RHIC), the National Synchrotron Light Source II (NSLS-II), and the Center for Functional Nanomaterials (CFN). The partnership between SBU and BNL enables joint faculty appointments, graduate student research opportunities, and collaborative projects in physics, chemistry, biology, and energy science.

title: Stony Brook University — $500 Million Simons Foundation Gift
clean_text: In 2023, the Simons Foundation pledged $500 million to Stony Brook University, making it the largest donation ever made to a public university in the United States. The gift was made in honor of the late James Simons, mathematician and founder of Renaissance Technologies hedge fund, who had a long relationship with SBU where he chaired the mathematics department. The donation will fund the Simons Institute for Science and Innovation (SISI) at SBU, which aims to advance fundamental research in mathematics, physics, and life sciences. The Simons Center for Geometry and Physics, already established on campus, continues to be a world-class research center. The gift is expected to significantly enhance SBU's research capabilities and global reputation over the coming decades.
